Unlocking TN Waterways
Overview Background Tasks Tools Priorities Timetable Team
TDOT Research Proposal · RFP-14

Unlocking Tennessee's Waterways: Multimodal Strategies for Freight Efficiency

Operational frameworks for multimodal freight integration — connecting Tennessee industries to domestic and international markets through the Tennessee and Cumberland River corridors. Building on RES2023-07 and RES2025-06 to transition from diagnosis to implementation.

$299.9KBudget
30 moTimeline
6Research Tasks
Project Overview
Why This Research Matters

Tennessee's inland waterways remain one of the state's most underutilized freight transportation assets. While highways and railroads face mounting congestion, the Tennessee and Cumberland Rivers provide direct connections to the Great Lakes and the Gulf of Mexico — a natural, cost-effective, and sustainable freight corridor. The state captures only a fraction of the waterway freight volume its navigable system can support, driving growing truck traffic on congested highways, higher emissions and logistics costs, and added strain on infrastructure. This research transitions from diagnostic analysis to implementation — quantitative and qualitative site-selection and network tools, improved origin-destination data, behavior-informed modal-shift analysis, coordinated corridor planning, and funding & governance strategy — to strengthen Tennessee's investment competitiveness and federal grant readiness.

$299.9K
Total Research Budget
30 mo

Prior TDOT Studies (RES2023-07, RES2025-06)

Integrated Research Tasks
Building On Prior Work
From Diagnosis to Implementation

This research operationalizes findings from two completed TDOT-funded projects, transitioning from feasibility analysis to actionable frameworks and investment-ready tools.

RES2023-07 ↗

Evaluated feasibility of shifting freight from highway and rail to waterways. Produced commodity-level freight flow analysis, stakeholder SWOT assessments, and an initial web-based decision-support tool.

RES2025-06

Identified significant untapped diversion potential along the Tennessee and Cumberland River corridors. Confirmed a structural imbalance between available waterway capacity and freight demand, and showed that strategically developed intermodal facilities — particularly in East Tennessee — could anchor industry clusters. Documented fragmented coordination as a key barrier.

This Project — RFP-14

Transitions to implementation: multimodal market opportunity analysis, port opportunity profiles, ranked hub screening, behavior-informed modal-shift microsimulation, implementation guidance, and a funding & governance strategy to strengthen investment competitiveness and federal grant readiness.

Research Plan
Six Integrated Tasks

Building on RES2023-07 and RES2025-06, the research moves from freight-market opportunity analysis through port and hub evaluation, behavior-informed modal-shift microsimulation, implementation guidance, and funding & governance strategy.

TASK 1

Translate Freight Flow Data into Multimodal Market Opportunities

Translate public and private freight data into a practical view of multimodal market opportunities physically, operationally, and economically compatible with inland waterway transport. Establish a freight baseline using FAF and ORNL tools, overlay demand patterns with multimodal infrastructure, and use UTK’s Global Supply Chain Institute (GSCI) network for stakeholder interviews and stated-preference survey design. Screen candidate flows for waterway compatibility to focus TDOT on opportunities that support business development.

1.1Freight baseline — FAF, ORNL-supported tools, state/regional and economic-development data
1.2Compile O-D flows, commodity intensity, shipper concentrations & industrial land uses
1.3Overlay demand with highways, rail, waterways, terminals, ports & industrial sites
1.4Waterway-compatibility screening: shipment size, distance, handling, drayage, proximity
1.5GSCI stakeholder interviews, stated-preference survey design & respondent recruitment
1.6Economic-development site/corridor identification & freight opportunity zones
FAF + ORNLO-D FlowsWaterway ScreeningGSCI NetworkOpportunity Dataset
TASK 2

Strengthen the Multimodal Role of Existing Ports

Evaluate existing Tennessee ports (and select private terminals) as multimodal connectors among barge, truck, and rail. Assess each port’s landside access, handling characteristics, waterway and rail proximity, adjacent land, and surrounding freight-generating industries. Explicitly evaluate connectivity gaps — first-mile/last-mile bottlenecks, transload limits, rail constraints — and identify the coordination and operating relationships needed to expand multimodal activity.

2.1Inventory Tennessee ports and select private terminals in their regional freight context
2.2Assess current & potential multimodal role: handling, highway/rail access, waterway, land
2.3Connectivity-gap assessment — access constraints, transload, rail, congestion, permitting
2.4Identify coordination & operating relationships (operators, carriers, railroads, 3PLs, EDOs)
2.5Port Opportunity Profiles linking conditions to surrounding freight market
Port ProfilesLandside AccessConnectivity GapsCoordinationPrivate Terminals
TASK 3

Screen Candidate Multimodal Hub Locations

Identify and rank candidate hub locations where freight-generating land uses, port or rail access, highway connectivity, and suitable operating conditions coincide. Combine GIS-based screening and feasibility filters (access, land, labor, water/energy, climate, permitting) with a structured cost-minimization evaluation of generalized logistics costs. Quantify potential congestion, safety, emissions, and infrastructure-preservation benefits using documented, transparent assumptions.

3.1GIS-based candidate-site identification at freight / port-rail / industrial convergence
3.2Feasibility filters: access, land, labor, water & energy, climate/resilience, permitting
3.3Cost-minimization evaluation: line-haul, drayage, transfer, handling, time, reliability
3.4Benefit quantification — congestion, safety, emissions, infrastructure preservation
3.5Ranked hub opportunities with operating concepts & recommended next-step actions
GIS ScreeningCost MinimizationBenefit EstimationSite Ranking
TASK 4

Assess Modal Shift Using Behavior-Informed Microsimulation

Apply proof-of-concept runs of MultiFreightSim (Golias 2026) — a proprietary behavior-informed microsimulation of regional freight flows across truck, rail, and barge and three routing scenarios (truck-only, truck-hub-barge, truck-hub-rail). For each potential shipment, the model assesses line-haul, transfer, drayage, and in-transit inventory costs, then lets shippers choose mode and route by cost and preference with congestion feedback. A new multimodal hub creates routing options absent from the input data, enabling out-of-sample assessment of how a candidate gateway re-shapes flows. Results are preliminary; the prototype requires further development beyond this project.

4.1Condense FAF / TransSearch flows into a list of potential shipments (tons, value, commodity)
4.2Compute per-mode/route costs: line-haul, transfer, drayage, in-transit inventory, reliability
4.3Shipper mode & route choice from costs + preference parameters, with congestion feedback
4.4Scenario testing of candidate hubs along the Tennessee, Cumberland & Mississippi rivers
4.5Benchmark against prior microeconomic navigational analysis (Maier et al. 2026)
MultiFreightSimTruck / Rail / BargeMode ChoiceScenario TestingProof-of-Concept
TASK 5

Translate Findings into Planning & Implementation Guidance

Synthesize the freight-market analysis, port profiles, hub ranking, cost comparisons, benefit estimates, and modal-shift results into practical guidance for TDOT decision-making. Organize findings into an implementation framework that separates near-term opportunities from longer-term strategic investments by market potential, infrastructure readiness, complexity, partner needs, benefits, and risk. Outline pilot concepts and partnership structures for the most promising opportunities, delivered as decision-ready graphics, maps, profiles, and ranking tables.

5.1Synthesize market, port, hub, cost, benefit & modal-shift findings into one framework
5.2Implementation framework: near- vs long-term, readiness, complexity, partners, benefits, risk
5.3Pilot concepts & partnership structures — commodities, partners, gaps, success measures
5.4Knowledge transfer: summary graphics, maps, profiles, ranking tables & executive summary
Implementation RoadmapPilot ConceptsPartnershipsKnowledge Transfer
TASK 6

Funding Mechanisms & Corridor-Scale Governance

Identify funding mechanisms and governance models that can move Tennessee multimodal opportunities from analysis to implementation. Building on RES2025-06 governance analysis, examine structures used in comparable inland-waterway and multimodal systems through qualitative interviews of port and terminal operators. Assess public, private, nonprofit, and federal funding sources — including PIDP, INFRA, and RAISE — for fit, eligibility, and readiness, and prepare a governance options matrix tailored to Tennessee’s institutional context.

6.1Review governance models: port authorities, landlord, P3, state-led, nonprofit, coalitions
6.2Qualitative interviews of comparable inland / river / Great-Lakes-connected port operations
6.3Funding mechanisms: federal discretionary (PIDP/INFRA/RAISE), state, private, bonds, P3
6.4Governance options matrix — authority, funding flexibility, risk, TN transferability
Funding MechanismsGovernance ModelsP3Options MatrixFederal Grants
Project Deliverables
Decision-Support Tools
KEY DELIVERABLES

Four complementary tools are developed as core project deliverables — a reusable GIS site-selection toolbox, an integrated multimodal freight database, a standalone Excel decision tool for non-GIS users, and the MultiFreightSim behavior-informed microsimulation prototype behind the Task 4 modal-shift assessment.

GIS Site-Selection Toolbox

ArcGIS Pro — TN_MultimodalHub_Screening.aprx

A reusable ArcGIS Pro toolbox TDOT can re-run with updated data or adjusted parameters. Sequential tools walk analysts from raw infrastructure inputs through site screening, attribute measurement, reclassification, weighted scoring, and scenario testing.

  • 1. Site Screening — filters by distance to waterway, rail, and interstate; minimum contiguous developable acres; FEMA flood zones; NWI wetlands
  • 2. Attribute Measurement — quantifies freight demand, modal connectivity scores, and labor-market indicators for each candidate
  • 3. Reclassification — normalizes raw attributes to a common 1–9 scale for cross-criteria comparison
  • 4. Weighted Scoring — applies AHP-derived criteria weights to produce composite suitability scores
  • 5. Scenario & Sensitivity Analysis — tests alternative weight sets; outputs ranked site maps (green→red composite scores)

Integrated Multimodal Freight Database

tn_multimodal_freight.db — integrated layers
O-D flows · commodity
tons · value · mode
ports & terminals
rail / highway / waterway
industrial sites
econ-dev zones

A single integrated database joining freight demand, infrastructure, and economic-development layers — the analytical backbone shared across Tasks 1–5 and reusable for TDOT’s freight planning.

  • Demand — FAF + ORNL-supported flows and (where available) disaggregated O-D by commodity, mode, tons, and value
  • Infrastructure — highways, rail, navigable waterways, ports, terminals, and industrial sites
  • Port attributes — landside access, handling, connectivity gaps from the Task 2 profiles
  • Reusable & transparent — documented sources and assumptions TDOT can update as new data arrives

A portable, Excel-based tool for non-GIS users — TDOT leadership, legislators, and MPO directors. Takes the hub-by-criteria matrix from the GIS analysis as fixed inputs and enables three functions without GIS expertise.

  • What-If Weight Analysis — adjust AHP criterion weights; rankings update automatically
  • Side-by-Side Scenario Comparison — baseline, budget-constrained, and regional-equity scenarios in a printable format
  • One-Page Hub Summaries — auto-generated profiles with scores by criterion, strengths/risks, and freight-demand context

MultiFreightSim — Behavior-Informed Microsimulation

multifreightsim — run --scenario truck-hub-barge
// 6,131 shipment agents · NOLA → Memphis demo
step 1 list shipments tons + $/ton + commodity
step 2 skim 3 routings truck / hub-barge / hub-rail
step 3 add delays queue + river events
step 4 logit choice generalized cost, θ≈$300
modal split  truck 26.9% · barge 40.6% · rail 32.6%
// illustrative synthetic demo — not a calibrated forecast

A behavior-informed mode-choice microsimulation that models freight one shipment at a time across truck, barge, and rail — the engine behind the Task 4 modal-shift assessment. Used here as a proof-of-concept prototype.

  • Shipment agents — disaggregates FAF / TransSearch totals into truckload-sized loads tagged by tons, value, and commodity
  • Generalized cost — line-haul + transfer + drayage + in-transit inventory + a value-of-reliability penalty
  • Logit choice with congestion feedback — shippers pick mode/route by cost and preference, not a forced cheapest
  • Sensitivity & tipping points — delivers curves and a minimum-subsidy table, not a single point forecast
  • New-gateway analysis — tests routing options a candidate hub creates that are absent from the input data
TDOT Research Strategic Priorities
How This Research Advances TDOT's Agenda

This research directly advances four of TDOT's Research Strategic Priorities, with measurable contributions to safety, preservation, technology, and organizational capacity.

P4

Multimodal Mobility Solutions Benefiting Safety & the Tennessee Economy

Identifies specific origin-destination pairs where freight can shift from road to waterways, quantifying reductions in crash exposure and logistics costs for Tennessee shippers.

P2

Security, Preservation & Enhancement of the Existing System

Estimates the physical strain on infrastructure and congestion costs from truck traffic that could move by barge — a data-driven basis for infrastructure preservation through modal shift.

P1

Emerging & Enabling Transportation Technologies Implementation

Delivers a GIS site-selection toolbox, an integrated multimodal freight database, an Excel decision-support tool, and the MultiFreightSim microsimulation — reusable analytical infrastructure for TDOT's freight planning.

P3

Organizational Transformation

Introduces a reusable, structured decision framework with weighted site selection and scenario analysis, plus governance-model assessment and an implementation plan to support pursuit of competitive public and private funding.

Project Schedule
30-Month Research Timetable

27 months of active research across six integrated tasks, followed by approximately 3 months of TDOT review, revisions, and final approval. Tasks overlap so that downstream analysis (hub screening, modal-shift microsimulation) builds on completed upstream work.

Research Team
Principal Investigators

Donald Maier

Principal Investigator — University of Tennessee, Knoxville

Lead investigator. Freight-market opportunity analysis, hub site-selection framework, and decision-support tool development.

Jeffrey Trombly

Co-PI — UTK

GIS analysis, site-screening methodology, and spatial decision-support tool development.

Andy Balthrop

Co-PI — UTK

Freight economics, corridor evaluation, benefit estimation, and the prior microeconomic navigational analysis.

Mihalis Golias

Co-PI — IAMPE

MultiFreightSim behavior-informed microsimulation, multimodal network modeling, and modal-shift / diversion analysis.

David Arnold

Co-PI — IAMPE, Executive Director

Intermodal operations, port opportunity profiles, governance & institutional framework analysis, and stakeholder coordination.
